# Generative grammar


<!-- foundations -->
```dataviewjs
dv.view("_dv/foundations")
```
<!-- /foundations -->

## summary

*Generative grammar* is a linguistic theory proposing that language is generated by a finite set of innate universal principles and rules operating on abstract structures. Developed by *Noam Chomsky* beginning in the 1950s, it revolutionized linguistics by shifting focus from describing observable patterns to explaining how the human mind produces and understands novel sentences.

## you gotta know

- Proposes that humans are born with an innate *universal grammar*—core principles shared by all human languages.
- Explains how speakers produce and understand *infinite* sentences using *finite* means (the "productivity" of language).
- Distinguishes between *deep structure* (abstract, underlying meaning) and *surface structure* (actual sentence form), connected by *transformational rules*.
- Accounts for *grammaticality judgments*—the fact that speakers intuitively know what is or is not a grammatical sentence, without being taught explicit rules.
- *Chomsky's Poverty of the Stimulus argument*: children acquire language so quickly and accurately that they must have innate constraints on hypothesis space.
- Rivals structural linguistics and behaviorism by rejecting the idea that language is simply learned through imitation and conditioning.
- Later developments include *X-bar theory*, *Government and Binding*, and *Minimalism*—increasingly abstract frameworks for syntax.

## connections

- [[linguistics]] — the discipline revolutionized by generative approaches.
- [[noam-chomsky]] — originator of transformational-generative grammar and the nativist program.
- [[structural-linguistics]] — the predecessor framework that Chomsky critiqued and largely superseded.
- [[cognitive-science]] — generative grammar's emphasis on mental rules and principles aligned with cognitive science's emergence.
- [[b-f-skinner]] — behaviorist whose account of language learning Chomsky famously rejected.

## see also

- [[linguistics]] · [[noam-chomsky]] · [[structural-linguistics]] · [[cognitive-science]]
